metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the config key path: either place `overlap_drain` inside the existing `[verify]` section (alongside `enable_code_review`) and update the env var to `REBAR_VERIFY_OVERLAP_DRAIN`, OR explicitly specify adding a new `[overlap]` section dataclass + entries in `_SECTION_CLASSES`, `_SECTIONS`, and `Config` in `_config_schema.py` — as-written `overlap.drain` will be silently dropped by `coerce_sparse()`.
findings.finding: The plan's opt-out config key `overlap.drain` implies a new top-level `[overlap]` TOML section, but `overlap` is not registered in `_SECTION_CLASSES` or `_SECTIONS` in `_config_schema.py`. The current config machinery (`coerce_sparse`) warns and silently drops any unregistered section — meaning the `overlap.drain = "off"` config knob would be silently ignored rather than respected, making the opt-out non-functional.
findings.location: Scope section: 'overlap.drain in VerifyConfig (src/rebar/_config_schema.py, [tool.rebar][verify], new overlap_* fields alongside require_plan_review_for_claim/enable_code_review)'; AC line: '[agents]/API key absent → clean no-op; overlap.drain=off disables the opportunistic drain'
findings.suggested_fix: Move the field into `VerifyConfig` as `overlap_drain: str = "on"` with a coercer `lambda v, k: _as_choice(v, k, {"on", "off"})` in `_SECTIONS["verify"]`, and use env override `REBAR_VERIFY_OVERLAP_DRAIN` for consistency with the existing `REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW` pattern. Update all plan references from `overlap.drain` → `verify.overlap_drain`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `overlap.drain` is a `verify.overlap_drain` key (no new section, env `REBAR_VERIFY_OVERLAP_DRAIN` following the established `REBAR_VERIFY_*` convention) or a genuinely new `[overlap]` top-level section — and if the latter, justify why a new section rather than adding to `[verify]` where the other gate toggles live.
findings.finding: The plan uses the notation `overlap.drain = "off"` (and env `REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN`), implying a new top-level `[overlap]` config section, while simultaneously describing the field as living inside `VerifyConfig` alongside `require_plan_review_for_claim` / `enable_code_review` (which are `verify.*` keys). No `[overlap]` section exists anywhere in the codebase. If this is a new top-level section it proliferates config surface for a single key; if it belongs under `[verify]` it should be `verify.overlap_drain` with env `REBAR_VERIFY_OVERLAP_DRAIN` (the established naming convention: `enable_code_review` → `REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW`).
findings.location: Scope section: 'overlap.drain in VerifyConfig (`src/rebar/_config_schema.py`, `[tool.rebar][verify]`, new `overlap_*` fields alongside `require_plan_review_for_claim`/`enable_code_review`'; What section: 'Opt-out via the TYPED config `overlap.drain = "off"` (env override `REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Place the key under the existing `verify` section as `verify.overlap_drain` with env `REBAR_VERIFY_OVERLAP_DRAIN`, matching the `enable_code_review` / `REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW` precedent. This avoids a new config section for a single key and stays consistent with the established naming convention.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the config-placement contradiction: decide whether `overlap.drain` is (a) a field on VerifyConfig under `[tool.rebar.verify]` (then the env var should be `REBAR_VERIFY_OVERLAP_DRAIN` to follow the existing convention) or (b) a new top-level `[tool.rebar.overlap]` section (then update the Scope text to say 'add an OverlapConfig dataclass and register it in _SECTIONS/_SECTION_CLASSES' and update VerifyConfig reference).
findings.finding: The plan locates 'overlap.drain' inside VerifyConfig in `_config_schema.py` alongside `require_plan_review_for_claim`/`enable_code_review` — implying it becomes a field on the existing `[verify]` section — but the env var name `REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN` implies a separate `[overlap]` section. These are mutually inconsistent: either the field is `verify.overlap_drain` (env `REBAR_VERIFY_OVERLAP_DRAIN`, matching enable_code_review → REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW) or it is `overlap.drain` (a new top-level section). The actual `_config_schema.py` has no `overlap` section in `_SECTIONS` or `_SECTION_CLASSES`.
findings.location: Scope section — 'overlap.drain in VerifyConfig (src/rebar/_config_schema.py, [tool.rebar][verify], new overlap_* fields alongside require_plan_review_for_claim/enable_code_review'; also 'What' — 'Opt-out via the TYPED config overlap.drain = "off"'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `overlap.drain` config-placement contradiction: either (a) rename the key to `verify.overlap_drain` and add it as a field in `VerifyConfig`, or (b) define an explicit new `OverlapConfig` dataclass and enumerate all required edits to `_SECTION_CLASSES`, `_SECTIONS`, `Config`, and `_RESERVED_SECTIONS` in `_config_schema.py` as named Scope targets.
findings.finding: The plan adds `overlap.drain` (TOML key `[overlap].drain`) but conflates it with `VerifyConfig` — the existing `[verify]` section dataclass. A new top-level `[overlap]` config section requires a new `OverlapConfig` dataclass, a new `Config` field, and registration in `_SECTION_CLASSES`, `_SECTIONS`, and `coerce_sparse` in `_config_schema.py`. None of these are named as edit targets. Alternatively, if the intent is to place `overlap_drain` as a field inside `VerifyConfig`, the TOML path `overlap.drain` is wrong. The plan is internally contradictory on which section owns this key.
findings.location: Scope section: '`overlap.drain` in `VerifyConfig` (`src/rebar/_config_schema.py`, `[tool.rebar][verify]`, new `overlap_*` fields alongside `require_plan_review_for_claim`/`enable_code_review`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Option (a) is simpler: rename the config key to `[tool.rebar][verify] overlap_drain = "on"` (consistent with `enable_code_review`, `require_plan_review_for_claim` naming pattern), add a `verify_overlap_drain: str = 'on'` field to `VerifyConfig`, and register its coercer in `_SECTIONS['verify']`. Update the env override name to `REBAR_VERIFY_OVERLAP_DRAIN` to match the existing `REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW` pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the config namespace: either (a) add `overlap_drain` to `VerifyConfig` with TOML key `[tool.rebar.verify] overlap_drain` and document as `verify.overlap_drain`, or (b) create a new `OverlapConfig` / `[tool.rebar.overlap]` section — then use the chosen dotted name consistently in both the Scope and every AC reference.
findings.finding: The plan names the config key 'overlap.drain = "off"' (Scope section and AC), which implies a separate [tool.rebar.overlap] TOML section, but simultaneously says it lives as 'overlap_*' fields in VerifyConfig under [tool.rebar.verify]. These are two structurally different TOML paths; the implementer cannot satisfy both. The existing codebase pattern (sync.push → SyncConfig, verify.enable_code_review → VerifyConfig) makes the section name the TOML key prefix, so 'overlap.drain' would require a new OverlapConfig/[overlap] section, while placing it in VerifyConfig means the TOML key would be [tool.rebar.verify] overlap_drain.
findings.location: Scope section ('overlap.drain in VerifyConfig ... [tool.rebar][verify], new overlap_* fields') vs AC line 4 ('overlap.drain=off')
findings.suggested_fix: Pick one: 'verify.overlap_drain' (field in VerifyConfig, env REBAR_VERIFY_OVERLAP_DRAIN, matching existing gate-config pattern) — update all Scope and AC references to use this exact dotted path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `overlap.drain` placement contradiction: choose either (a) a new `OverlapConfig` dataclass with TOML section `[tool.rebar.overlap]` registered in `_SECTION_CLASSES`, or (b) a flat `verify.overlap_drain` field in `VerifyConfig` — and update all references (Scope, What, ACs, env-var name) to use the chosen TOML path consistently.
findings.finding: The `overlap.drain` config key's schema placement is internally contradictory: the Scope section places it as `overlap_*` fields inside `VerifyConfig` (`[tool.rebar][verify]`, alongside `require_plan_review_for_claim`/`enable_code_review`), while the key name `overlap.drain` implies a separate `[tool.rebar.overlap]` TOML section. Neither placement exists today, and the two instructions are incompatible — an executor cannot satisfy both.
findings.location: Scope section: 'overlap.drain in VerifyConfig (`src/rebar/_config_schema.py`, `[tool.rebar][verify]`, new `overlap_*` fields alongside `require_plan_review_for_claim`/`enable_code_review`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the two unenumerated CLI wiring targets to the Scope: (1) an `enrich` intercept arm in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` (pattern: `if argv and argv[0] == 'enrich': return _enrich(argv[1:])`), and (2) a `src/rebar/_cli/help/enrich.txt` package-data file with the subcommand usage string.
findings.finding: The plan names only `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py` as the CLI edit target for `rebar enrich`, but the actual subcommand dispatch architecture requires two additional unenumerated edit targets: (1) `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` — every native subcommand (review-plan, workflow, llm, etc.) has an intercept arm here; without it `rebar enrich` falls through to the unknown-subcommand gate and exits with an error; (2) `src/rebar/_cli/help/enrich.txt` — `known_subcommands()` is driven entirely by this package-data directory, and an absent `enrich.txt` causes `rebar enrich --help` to emit 'unknown subcommand' rather than usage.
findings.location: Scope section: 'CLI arm in `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the injection point ambiguity in Scope: replace '(or to `review_plan`/`claim`)' with a definitive list of the rebar commands/code paths that will call `maybe_drain()`, so affected callers are explicitly acknowledged and the latency guarantee is scoped correctly.
findings.finding: The injection point for `maybe_drain()` is left unresolved by an 'or': 'attached to the same post-write path as push (or to `review_plan`/`claim`)'. This means it is undefined which existing command paths will grow the gate latency, making AC #2's latency guarantee unverifiable and leaving implementers to decide scope of behavioral change without guidance.
findings.location: Scope section — 'the opportunistic hook attached to the same post-write path as push (or to `review_plan`/`claim`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Enumerate the exact entry points, e.g.: '`maybe_drain()` is called at the end of `claim`, `review_plan`, and any command that writes to the store — the same post-write hook used by `push`. It is NOT called on read-only commands (status, log, etc.).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a claim-expiry / reclaim policy: specify a TTL on claimed tickets (e.g., reclaim after N minutes if no DONE_ENRICH) or an unclaim-on-crash path, and add a test for the child-crash recovery scenario.
findings.finding: If the detached drain child crashes (OOM, SIGKILL, unhandled exception) after claiming a ticket but before appending `DONE_ENRICH`, the ticket is left permanently in the 'claimed' state with no expiry, unclaim, or dead-letter path specified. There is no RETIRE/cleanup mechanism defined for this partial state.
findings.location: What section — drainer loop description; AC line 3 (concurrent drains); Dependencies note
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `claimed_at` timestamp to the claim record and document that `pending_enrichment()` (S3) treats claims older than `CLAIM_EXPIRY_MINUTES` (e.g., 10 min) as timed-out and re-queues them. Reference this in the AC and add a unit test: `test_stale_claim_is_reclaimed`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the concrete hook call site(s) in the Scope: specify whether `maybe_drain()` is called from `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` (after every write, like push), from `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py` (`claim_cli`), or from `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` (post-dispatch); add the chosen file(s) as named edit targets.
findings.finding: The plan says the opportunistic `maybe_drain()` hook is 'attached to the same post-write path as push' but does not enumerate `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` as an edit target. This is the concrete call site where `push_tickets_branch` is invoked after every locked commit (confirmed at line 189 of `event_append.py`). Adding `maybe_drain()` there is a behavioral change to an existing file outside the new artifact's directory, and it is a required consumer update.
findings.location: Scope section: 'the opportunistic hook attached to the same post-write path as push (or to `review_plan`/`claim`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the unverified capability assertion: confirm whether `subprocess.DETACHED_PROCESS` is available as a named constant in the target Python versions, or specify that the plan requires `DETACHED_PROCESS = 0x00000008` as a locally-defined constant (since CPython does not export this name from `subprocess`).
findings.finding: `subprocess.DETACHED_PROCESS` is not a named constant in CPython's subprocess module. The plan specifies `creationflags=DETACHED_PROCESS|CREATE_NO_WINDOW` for the Windows detach path, implying `subprocess.DETACHED_PROCESS` is usable by name. In reality, only `subprocess.CREATE_NO_WINDOW` (added Python 3.7) and `subprocess.CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP` are stdlib-exported. `DETACHED_PROCESS` (value 0x00000008) must be used as a raw integer literal or locally-defined constant.
findings.location: What — Detach mechanism, cross-platform: 'Windows uses `creationflags=DETACHED_PROCESS|CREATE_NO_WINDOW`'
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's Windows detach spec, replace the bare name reference with an explicit note: `DETACHED_PROCESS = 0x00000008` must be defined locally (it is not exported by `subprocess`), while `subprocess.CREATE_NO_WINDOW` is available from Python 3.7+. Example: `creationflags = 0x00000008 | subprocess.CREATE_NO_WINDOW`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-handling contract in the drainer loop: on S1 failure, define whether the ticket is (a) unclaimed and re-queued, (b) marked `FAILED_ENRICH` with logged detail, or (c) retried with backoff — and ensure the log entry captures the exception.
findings.finding: The plan specifies the drainer runs `enrich (S1)` but does not define what happens to ticket state when S1 raises an exception (LLM timeout, rate-limit, malformed response). The ticket may be left claimed-but-not-done, or the drain may silently exit mid-batch with no record of the failure mode.
findings.location: What section — drainer loop; AC line 1 (`rebar enrich --drain` processes up to DRAIN_BATCH entries)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the drainer loop spec: 'on S1 exception, log the full traceback to enrich-drain.log, mark the ticket `FAILED_ENRICH` (or unclaim it), and continue to the next ticket in the batch rather than aborting the batch.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `test_drain_enrich_throws` test (and corresponding AC) specifying the drainer's behavior when S1 raises mid-batch: e.g., log the error, release/skip the claim, and continue to the next entry — verifying the queue is not left in a corrupted claimed state.
findings.finding: The `--drain` batch-processing flow (a new user-facing behavior) has no test for the failure path where S1 `enrich` raises an exception mid-batch. The plan names FakeRunner for end-to-end coverage but never specifies what the drainer does when one entry's enrich call throws — whether it logs and continues, aborts the batch, or corrupts the queue state. This is the most realistic production failure scenario (LLM call timeout, network error, malformed S3 entry) and its absence leaves the error-recovery contract undefined and untested.
findings.location: Testing section / AC list (no AC covers enrich-throws mid-drain)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Testing section: `enrich raises on entry N → logged to enrich-drain.log, claim released (or skipped), remaining entries processed up to DRAIN_BATCH`. Add a corresponding AC: 'When enrich throws during a drain batch, the drainer logs the error, does not leave the entry permanently claimed, and continues processing remaining entries.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a named error-handling protocol for S1 enrich failures in the drainer loop (e.g. 'on exception: log to enrich-drain.log, skip ticket, continue to next in batch') and add a corresponding test case to the Testing section.
findings.finding: The drainer loop specifies no fallback or error-handling protocol for an individual `enrich` (S1) call failure (exception, LLM API error, timeout). The processing sequence is stated as claim → enrich → write TICKET_DIGEST → append DONE_ENRICH, but what happens when the enrich step raises is left entirely unspecified.
findings.location: What section: 'The drainer loop: from `pending_enrichment(now)` (S3), claim one (locked read-check-append, S3), run `enrich` (S1), write `TICKET_DIGEST` (S2), append `DONE_ENRICH`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the drainer loop description: 'on S1 exception: log the error and ticket id to `.rebar/logs/enrich-drain.log`, skip to the next ticket (do NOT write TICKET_DIGEST or DONE_ENRICH), continue the batch.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define DRAIN_BATCH: specify its default value, whether it is a module-level constant or config/env-overridable, and where it lives (e.g., `enrich_drain.py` constant, defaulting to N, optionally overridden by `REBAR_DRAIN_BATCH`).
findings.finding: `DRAIN_BATCH` is referenced as a named constant in both the What section and AC #1 but its default value, whether it is configurable (config key, env var, or hardcoded), and where it is defined are never specified in the plan.
findings.location: What section ('process UP TO DRAIN_BATCH tickets per run'); AC #1 ('processes up to DRAIN_BATCH soaked+unclaimed entries')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default value for `DRAIN_BATCH` (e.g. `DRAIN_BATCH = 5`) and specify whether it is a hardcoded module constant or a user-configurable setting (env var / config key); update AC-1 to reference the concrete default.
findings.finding: `DRAIN_BATCH` is referenced as a named constant throughout the plan (drainer loop description, AC-1, Testing section) but no default value is ever stated. An executor writing `enrich_drain.py` must invent an integer with no guidance.
findings.location: What section ('process UP TO `DRAIN_BATCH` tickets per run'); AC-1 ('rebar enrich --drain processes up to `DRAIN_BATCH` soaked+unclaimed entries'); Testing section ('`--drain` respects `DRAIN_BATCH` and exits')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What or Scope section: '`DRAIN_BATCH` defaults to 5 (a module-level constant in `enrich_drain.py`); not user-configurable in v1.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 'or' and commit to a single hook attachment point (e.g. 'same post-write path as push, as the primary; review_plan/claim as a fallback hook — pick one and state it').
findings.finding: The opportunistic hook attachment point is described with an unresolved 'or': 'the same post-write path as push (or to `review_plan`/`claim`)' — this is a real design decision (which hook?) deferred to the executor without a stated default.
findings.location: ## Scope, hook bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the env override name with the REBAR_{SECTION}_{KEY} convention: if the field is in `[verify]`, use `REBAR_VERIFY_OVERLAP_DRAIN`; update the Scope, AC, and Testing sections consistently.
findings.finding: The env override name 'REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN' does not follow the codebase's REBAR_{SECTION}_{KEY} convention. The codebase uses REBAR_SYNC_PUSH for sync.push and REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW for verify.enable_code_review. If the key is placed in [verify], the env var should be REBAR_VERIFY_OVERLAP_DRAIN (or REBAR_VERIFY_DRAIN if named verify.drain); if in a new [overlap] section, REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN is correct but a new section must be created.
findings.location: Scope section and AC line 4: 'env override REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default value of `DRAIN_BATCH` (e.g. `DRAIN_BATCH = 5`) explicitly in the plan so AC-1 and the batch-cap test are unambiguous.
findings.finding: `DRAIN_BATCH` is referenced throughout (AC-1, drainer loop, testing section) but no default value is stated anywhere in the plan; an executing agent must invent a number.
findings.location: ## What (drainer loop bullet) / Acceptance Criteria AC-1
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line such as 'DRAIN_BATCH default: 5 (configurable via overlap.drain_batch in VerifyConfig)' to the ## What section.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that the Windows `creationflags` detach path is novel (not reused from push.py) and add a note on the Windows Job Object constraint: `DETACHED_PROCESS` alone does not guarantee child survival when the parent runs inside a Job Object (common in CI, IDEs, and some terminals); consider whether `CREATE_BREAKAWAY_FROM_JOB` is needed or explicitly document the known limitation.
findings.finding: The plan claims 'Reuse push.py's PYTHONPATH bootstrap' for the Windows detach path, but push.py has no Windows detach path at all — it only uses `start_new_session=True` (POSIX). The Windows `creationflags` branch is entirely novel to the codebase, yet the plan presents it as a reuse of an established pattern. This risks implementors assuming the Windows path was validated alongside push.py when it was not.
findings.location: What — Detach mechanism: 'Reuse push.py's PYTHONPATH bootstrap so the child can `import rebar`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the 'What / Detach mechanism' section that the plan DEPARTS from push.py on two points: (1) stderr must NOT be DEVNULL (keep it open to the log file) and (2) push.py has no Windows branch so the Windows creationflags arm is entirely new — update the 'Reuse push.py' phrasing to 'Reuse push.py's PYTHONPATH bootstrap only; diverge on stderr and add a Windows-specific branch'.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Reuse push.py's PYTHONPATH bootstrap' as the detach model, but push.py routes stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL (line 135) while the plan's new design deliberately diverges by keeping stderr open to a log file. The 'reuse push.py' framing and the stderr divergence are stated in the same breath without flagging that this is NOT a wholesale reuse — a developer following the push.py template will need to actively omit the stderr=DEVNULL kwarg, and push.py has no Windows branch despite being presented as the proven cross-platform model.
findings.location: What section — detach mechanism paragraph; also AC line 6 (cross-platform detach)
findings.suggested_fix: Change the detach paragraph to: 'Borrow push.py's PYTHONPATH + python -c bootstrap pattern (push.py lines 118-132) but diverge in two ways: (a) stderr goes to the rotating log, NOT DEVNULL; (b) push.py has no Windows branch — add one here with creationflags=DETACHED_PROCESS|CREATE_NO_WINDOW (branch on os.name == "nt").' Also update AC-6 to say 'push.py is the POSIX model only; the Windows path is new'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a smoke/integration test (or explicitly-documented manual proving command) for AC-2 that spawns a real child process, exits the parent, and verifies the child has written a sentinel to the log file, proving actual survival beyond parent exit.
findings.finding: AC-2 claims the detached child 'survives parent exit' but the only proving mechanism cited is a unit test asserting Popen kwargs — mocking Popen can verify arguments but cannot prove actual process survival past parent exit, leaving this core claim unverified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC-2
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC or integration test: `python -c 'import subprocess, sys; p = subprocess.Popen([sys.executable, "-m", "rebar", "enrich", "--drain"]); sys.exit(0)' && sleep 2 && grep -q DONE .rebar/logs/enrich-drain.log` (adapt to test harness), gated behind an explicit 'integration' pytest mark so it's opt-in in CI.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] (Advisory) Consider adding a contract/stub step: define the S3 queue/claim interface (even as a Python Protocol or stub) within this story or a shared prerequisite, so `enrich_drain.py` can be partially implemented and tested with a fake before all three prerequisites land.
findings.finding: All three prerequisite stories (S1 enrich op, S2 digest store, S3 queue+claim) are explicitly 'not existing code' and must ship before this story can be integrated or tested end-to-end. The plan has no thin vertical-slice or evidence-gated MVP that de-risks the riskiest dependency (S3 queue/claim locking) before full implementation; the entire story lands in one batch after all three prerequisites.
findings.location: Dependencies section — 'All three are prerequisite sibling stories under this epic, not existing code.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one integration/smoke AC (or explicitly defer to a named child ticket) that spawns a real detached child via `maybe_drain()`, exits the parent, and asserts the child process is still alive (e.g., via a sentinel file written by the child after a short sleep), verifying the cross-platform survival guarantee beyond flag-value inspection.
findings.finding: The newly-DEFAULTED opportunistic detach path (every ordinary rebar invocation can trigger `maybe_drain()` → `Popen(...)`) is validated ONLY through mock Popen kwargs inspection, never through an actual child-survives-parent-exit integration test. The AC states 'detaches a drain that survives parent exit' but no test actually spawns a real child, exits the parent, and confirms the child is still running. A mis-specified flag (or a platform where the flags don't guarantee orphan behavior) would pass the test suite while breaking the core guarantee.
findings.location: AC bullet 2: 'An ordinary invocation opportunistically detaches a drain that survives parent exit' / Testing section: 'Windows detach path unit-tested via os.name branch (mock Popen kwargs)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an integration test (can be marked `@pytest.mark.integration` and skipped in CI without credentials) that: (1) calls `maybe_drain()` with a seeded queue entry and a short-sleep FakeRunner child script, (2) immediately exits the parent process context, (3) polls for a sentinel file written by the child, confirming it ran after parent exit. Or explicitly state 'end-to-end survival verified in the S1/S2/S3 integration suite (named ticket X)' to invoke the named-child-ticket exemption.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and test the `--once` empty-queue behavior: specify the exit code (e.g., 0 with 'nothing to enrich' message, or a distinct exit code) and add `test_once_empty_queue` to the unit test plan.
findings.finding: `--once` behavior when the queue is empty is not specified: the AC says it 'claims+processes exactly one entry' but defines no outcome (exit code, message, or error) when there is nothing to claim. For a command intended as the primary test harness (`--once` is for tests), an undefined empty-queue behavior makes test setup brittle.
findings.location: AC line 5: '`--once` claims+processes exactly one entry'; Testing section: '`--once` processes one entry end-to-end (FakeRunner)'
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: '`--once` with empty queue exits 0 and prints `No soaked entries pending enrichment.` to stdout; `--once` with a non-empty queue exits 0 after processing and prints the ticket id enriched.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC #1/AC #5) covering idempotency: re-running `--once` (or `--drain`) on an already-processed entry produces the same content-hash result and does not append a duplicate event.
findings.finding: Idempotency of the drainer ('a re-run overwrites by content-hash') is described as a behavioral guarantee in the What section but has no corresponding acceptance criterion verifying it.
findings.location: What section ('Idempotent: a re-run overwrites by content-hash'); Acceptance Criteria (all six ACs)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the log path '.rebar/logs/enrich-drain.log' is resolved relative to the repo root (passed explicitly to the child via argv, following push.py's base_path pattern), and note this in the Scope/enrich_drain.py file description.
findings.finding: The plan says the detached child appends stderr to '.rebar/logs/enrich-drain.log' but does not specify what root this path is relative to. The child process is spawned detached with an inherited or unspecified CWD; push.py passes base_path explicitly as an argv argument so the child always knows the store root, but enrich_drain.py's equivalent anchor is not stated. Without grounding the log path to a known root (repo root? tracker dir? os.getcwd() at spawn time?), the log file may appear in an unexpected directory or fail to create.
findings.location: Observability section: 'it appends to a rotating log .rebar/logs/enrich-drain.log (git-ignored)'
findings.suggested_fix: Pass the repo root as a positional argument to the detached child (parallel to push.py's base_path argv[1]) and resolve the log path as os.path.join(repo_root, '.rebar', 'logs', 'enrich-drain.log').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete valid value vocabulary for `overlap.drain` (e.g. `"on" | "off"`, or `"always" | "async" | "off"` mirroring `sync.push`) in the Scope or What section, co-located with the schema definition.
findings.finding: The `overlap.drain` config key's valid value vocabulary is never fully defined. Only `"off"` is named as the opt-out. The modeling precedent (`sync.push`) is a 3-value enum (`always | async | off`) enforced by `_as_choice` in `_config_schema.py:449`, which requires an explicit set. An executor implementing the coercer cannot determine the other valid values.
findings.location: What section ('Opt-out via the TYPED config `overlap.drain = "off"`'); Scope section ('overlap.drain in VerifyConfig'); AC-4 ('overlap.drain=off ... disables the opportunistic drain entirely')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line such as: 'Valid values: `"on"` (default — opportunistic async drain) | `"off"` (disable drain entirely); the `sync.push` 3-value pattern does not apply here as there is no synchronous drain mode.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command or test name that exercises the multi-run backlog-drain scenario (e.g. `test_successive_runs_drain_backlog`: load DRAIN_BATCH+1 entries, run drain twice via `--once` loop, assert all entries reach DONE_ENRICH).
findings.finding: AC-1 claims 'a backlog > DRAIN_BATCH drains across successive runs' but the only proving command cited (`test_batch_cap`) tests a single run's batch cap — there is no proving command or test for the multi-run backlog-draining behavior.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC-1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the latency-assertion approach: name the measurement scope (wall-clock from `maybe_drain()` entry to return, with queue-empty precondition), and document that the test skips or uses a generous multiplier in CI to avoid flap (or mark it as a benchmark, not a hard assertion).
findings.finding: AC-2 asserts 'the foreground command's added latency is within DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS (default 20ms)' but provides no concrete measurement mechanism — a bare timing assertion in a unit test is timing-environment-sensitive and can silently pass on a fast developer machine while failing in slower CI.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC-2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a described test (or expand the AC #2 test spec) that measures the gate's foreground latency on the spawn path (when entries ARE soaked), not only the no-spawn path, to make the DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS bound verifiable.
findings.finding: The Testing section only describes testing the no-spawn gate path ('gate latency ~0 when nothing soaked (assert no detach spawned)'), but AC #2 asserts 'the foreground command's added latency is within DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS' as a pass/fail criterion — there is no described test for the latency bound on the spawn path (when something IS soaked and a child IS detached).
findings.location: Testing section ('gate latency ~0 when nothing soaked') vs AC #2 ('within DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS (default 20ms)')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Testing section, add: 'gate latency ≤ DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS even when a drain is spawned (mock Popen to return instantly; assert wall-clock delta from maybe_drain() call to return is within budget)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a microbenchmark or spike (even a single timing fixture against a local stub of S3) to derive or validate the 20ms DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS default, and update AC2's test to assert actual wall-clock gate latency rather than only checking 'no spawn'.
findings.finding: The 20ms DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS default is asserted without any measurement basis or profiling spike. The test asserts only 'no detach spawned' (a boolean outcome), not wall-clock latency against a real or simulated S3 backing store. On slow-path scenarios (cold FS, network-mounted repo, first-call overhead) this threshold may be violated with no data to justify or tune it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'within DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS (default 20ms)'; Testing section: 'gate latency ~0 when nothing soaked'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a pytest fixture that measures wall-clock time of maybe_drain() with an empty queue against a local file-backed S3 stub (10 iterations, take p95). Record the result as the basis for the 20ms default in a comment in the source. Update AC2's test to `assert elapsed_ms < DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting (a) the rationale for the opportunistic detached-drain pattern, (b) the POSIX vs Windows process-creation branching decision, and (c) the claim/lock protocol — before or as part of this story's delivery.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel cross-platform architectural pattern (opportunistic detached drain, cross-platform child-process orphan management, rotating log, new config section, new pipeline stage wiring S1/S2/S3) but does not mention capturing this decision in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or any design document. Future contributors encountering the detach mechanism, Windows branching, or the S3 claim protocol will have no authoritative record of why these choices were made.
findings.location: Scope section; What section (detach mechanism, observability, drainer loop); Dependencies section
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0XXX-opportunistic-detached-drain.md` covering: the npm update-notifier precedent, the Windows DETACHED_PROCESS requirement and why start_new_session is insufficient there, and the S3 claim idempotency contract. Reference it from the module docstring of enrich_drain.py.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
